subject: Neurology
front: Which cutaneous receptor is the most numerous and found only in non-hairy (glabrous) skin?
back: Meissner corpuscle

subject: Neurology
front: What functions does the Meissner corpuscle serve?
back: Detects fine touch, dynamic two-point discrimination, and low-frequency vibration

subject: Neurology
front: What type of adaptation does the Meissner corpuscle show?
back: Fast adaptation

subject: Neurology
front: Which receptor is the largest and most sensitive cutaneous receptor?
back: Pacinian corpuscle

subject: Neurology
front: What does the Pacinian corpuscle detect?
back: High-frequency vibration and deep pressure

subject: Neurology
front: What type of adaptation does the Pacinian corpuscle show?
back: Fast adaptation

subject: Neurology
front: What does the hair follicle receptor detect, and what is its adaptation type?
back: Detects hair movement; shows fast adaptation

subject: Neurology
front: Which slow-adapting receptor is located at epidermal edges and is important for Braille reading?
back: Merkel disc

subject: Neurology
front: What discrimination function is attributed to the Merkel disc?
back: Static two-point discrimination

subject: Neurology
front: Which slow-adapting receptor detects skin stretch and sustained pressure, and is maximal in joint capsules?
back: Ruffini endings

subject: Neurology
front: Which nerve endings mediate itching and slow pain, and which molecule mediates this?
back: Type C nerve endings, mediated by Substance P

subject: Neurology
front: Which neurotransmitter mediates fast pain (as opposed to the slow pain carried by Type C fibers)?
back: Glutamate

subject: Neurology
front: What mnemonic helps recall which receptors show fast adaptation?
back: "Miss Pacman is Fast" - referring to Meissner corpuscle, Pacinian corpuscle, and hair follicle receptors

subject: Neurology
front: State the Weber-Fechner law of perception.
back: As stimulus intensity increases, perception increases

subject: Neurology
front: State the frequency coding law of neural signaling.
back: As stimulus intensity increases, the frequency of action potentials increases

subject: Neurology
front: What are the functions, conduction velocity, and diameter of Aalpha nerve fibers?
back: Proprioception and somatic motor function; conduction velocity 70-120 m/sec; diameter 12-20 microns

subject: Neurology
front: What are the functions, conduction velocity, and diameter of Abeta nerve fibers?
back: Touch and pressure; conduction velocity 30-70 m/sec; diameter 8 microns

subject: Neurology
front: What are the functions, conduction velocity, and diameter of Agamma nerve fibers?
back: Efferent fibers to muscle spindles; conduction velocity 15-30 m/sec; diameter 5 microns

subject: Neurology
front: What are the functions, conduction velocity, and diameter of Adelta nerve fibers?
back: Fast pain (via glutamate) and cold temperature sensation; conduction velocity 12-30 m/sec; diameter 2-5 microns

subject: Neurology
front: What are the functions, conduction velocity, and diameter of Type B nerve fibers?
back: Preganglionic autonomic fibers; conduction velocity 3-12 m/sec; diameter 3 microns

subject: Neurology
front: What are the functions, conduction velocity, and diameter of Type C nerve fibers?
back: Slow pain, warm temperature sensation, and postganglionic sympathetic fibers; conduction velocity 0.5-2 m/sec; diameter 1 micron

subject: Neurology
front: Which fiber type is unmyelinated, and what is its alternate name in the Lloyd and Hunt classification?
back: Type C fibers; also known as Type 4 fibers

subject: Neurology
front: What sensations do Adelta fibers carry and via which neurotransmitter?
back: Cold temperature and fast/sharp pain, mediated by glutamate

subject: Neurology
front: What sensations do C fibers carry and via which neurotransmitter?
back: Warm/burning/freezing pain and slow pain, mediated by Substance P

subject: Neurology
front: What is the order of nerve fiber sensitivity to local anesthetics, and what mnemonic captures it?
back: A (Agamma > Aalpha > Adelta) > B > C; mnemonic "GoD!"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the order of nerve fiber sensitivity to pressure, and what mnemonic captures it?
back: A > B > C; mnemonic "Return to basics"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the order of nerve fiber sensitivity to hypoxia, and what mnemonic captures it?
back: B > A > C; mnemonic "BACo!"

subject: Neurology
front: How do conduction velocity and fiber diameter change from Aalpha fibers down to C fibers?
back: Both conduction velocity and fiber diameter progressively decrease from Aalpha to C fibers (larger diameter = faster conduction)

subject: Neurology
front: Which fiber types carry pain signals, and what pathway do they take to reach conscious perception?
back: Adelta fibers and C fibers carry pain via the spinothalamic tract to the brain, where it is perceived

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the descending pain inhibition system?
back: It suppresses nociceptive transmission in chronic pain at the level of the spinothalamic tract

subject: Neurology
front: What is the role of the periaqueductal gray (PAG) in pain modulation?
back: It activates descending inhibition pathways

subject: Neurology
front: Which structure and neurotransmitter inhibit pain at the spinal level via serotonergic pathways?
back: Nucleus raphe, using serotonin (5-HT)

subject: Neurology
front: Which structure and neurotransmitter inhibit pain at the spinal level via noradrenergic pathways?
back: Locus ceruleus, using norepinephrine (NE)

subject: Neurology
front: What do interneurons in the dorsal horn release to inhibit pain transmission?
back: Enkephalins and dynorphins

subject: Neurology
front: What is the Gate Control Theory of pain, and who proposed it?
back: Proposed by Melzack & Wall; activation of Abeta (touch) fibers inhibits Adelta and C fiber pain transmission

subject: Neurology
front: What structure is known as the "Gating Center" in the Gate Control Theory?
back: The substantia gelatinosa (lamina 2 of the dorsal horn)

subject: Neurology
front: Give two clinical applications of the Gate Control Theory of pain.
back: TENS (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation) and acupuncture (which increases enkephalin release)

subject: Neurology
front: Define hyperalgesia.
back: An exaggerated response to a painful stimulus

subject: Neurology
front: Define allodynia.
back: A non-painful stimulus that is perceived as painful

subject: Neurology
front: A woman gets sunburned on the beach, and later feels pain during a 40 degree C warm bath. Which receptor and phenomenon explain this pain?
back: Innocuous thermal receptors (not nociceptors, since 40 degree C is below the ~49 degree C nociceptor threshold) are activated; the pain represents allodynia due to sunburn sensitization

subject: Neurology
front: Where is acetylcholine synthesized, and how are its levels altered in depression and mania?
back: Synthesized in the basal nucleus of Meynert; increased in depression, decreased in mania

subject: Neurology
front: How are acetylcholine levels altered in Alzheimer's disease, Huntington's disease, and Parkinson's disease?
back: Markedly decreased in Alzheimer's disease, decreased in Huntington's disease, increased in Parkinson's disease

subject: Neurology
front: Where is dopamine synthesized, and how are its levels altered in depression, mania, and schizophrenia?
back: Synthesized in the ventral tegmentum and substantia nigra; decreased in depression, markedly increased in mania, markedly increased in schizophrenia

subject: Neurology
front: How are dopamine levels altered in Huntington's disease and Parkinson's disease?
back: Increased in Huntington's disease; markedly decreased in Parkinson's disease

subject: Neurology
front: Where is GABA synthesized, and how are its levels altered in anxiety, depression, and mania?
back: Synthesized in the nucleus accumbens; markedly decreased in anxiety, decreased in depression, decreased in mania

subject: Neurology
front: How is GABA altered in Huntington's disease?
back: Decreased

subject: Neurology
front: Where is norepinephrine synthesized, and how are its levels altered in anxiety and depression?
back: Synthesized in the locus ceruleus; markedly increased in anxiety, markedly decreased in depression

subject: Neurology
front: Where is serotonin synthesized, and how are its levels altered in anxiety and depression?
back: Synthesized in the raphe nuclei (medulla, pons); markedly decreased in both anxiety and depression

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the lateral hypothalamic nucleus, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: Mediates hunger and anger, stimulated by ghrelin; mnemonic "Makes you grow laterally"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the ventromedial hypothalamic nucleus, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: Mediates satiety, stimulated by leptin; mnemonic "Opposite of Lateral nucleus"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the anterior hypothalamic nucleus, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: Mediates cooling; mnemonic "AC - Anterior cooling"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the posterior hypothalamic nucleus, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: Mediates heat generation via shivering and vasoconstriction; mnemonic "Opposite of anterior"

subject: Neurology
front: What hormone does the supraoptic nucleus release, and what mnemonic covers this along with the paraventricular nucleus?
back: ADH; mnemonic "SAD POX"

subject: Neurology
front: What hormone does the paraventricular nucleus release?
back: Oxytocin (covered by the mnemonic "SAD POX")

subject: Neurology
front: What does the suprachiasmatic nucleus release, from which gland, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: Releases melatonin from the pineal gland; mnemonic "Circadian - Chiasmatic"

subject: Neurology
front: What hormone does the preoptic nucleus release, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: GnRH; mnemonic "Pre gonadotropin hormone"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the overall regulatory role of the arcuate nucleus?
back: It is the regulator center for hunger, satiety, and reproduction

subject: Neurology
front: Which neurons in the arcuate nucleus stimulate hunger?
back: NPY/AgRP neurons

subject: Neurology
front: Which neurons in the arcuate nucleus promote satiety?
back: POMC/CART neurons

subject: Neurology
front: What is the role of kisspeptin neurons in the arcuate nucleus?
back: They act as a trigger for puberty by stimulating pulsatile GnRH secretion

subject: Neurology
front: Why is pulsatile GnRH release stimulated by kisspeptin neurons important?
back: It is essential for proper activation of LH and FSH

subject: Neurology
front: What causes Kallmann syndrome, and what is its clinical consequence?
back: A mutation of kisspeptin, causing delayed or absent puberty

subject: Neurology
front: What is leptin's role in puberty?
back: It acts as a permissive signal for puberty - it allows puberty to happen but does not directly start it

subject: Neurology
front: What effect does low leptin (as seen in anorexia) have on puberty?
back: It causes delayed or suppressed puberty

subject: Neurology
front: What is the core body temperature set point?
back: 37 degrees C

subject: Neurology
front: What dominates thermoregulation when body temperature rises above 37 degrees C?
back: Evaporative heat loss (sweating) dominates

subject: Neurology
front: What dominates thermoregulation when body temperature falls below 37 degrees C?
back: Heat production dominates

subject: Neurology
front: What happens to the hypothalamic set point during fever, and how does the body respond?
back: The set point rises; shivering and vasoconstriction occur to raise body temperature to the new set point

subject: Neurology
front: What happens during the "crisis" (defervescence) phase when a fever resolves?
back: The set point suddenly drops, and vasodilation plus sweating occur to lower body temperature

subject: Neurology
front: List the anorexigenic (feeding-decreasing) mediators described in the notes.
back: alpha-MSH, Leptin, Serotonin, Norepinephrine, CRH, Insulin, Cholecystokinin (CCK), Glucagon-like peptide (GLP), CART, and Peptide YY (PYY)

subject: Neurology
front: List the orexigenic (feeding-increasing) mediators described in the notes.
back: Neuropeptide Y (NPY), Agouti-related protein (AGRP), Melanin-concentrating hormone (MCH), Orexins A and B, Endorphins, Galanin (GAL), Amino acids (glutamate and gamma-aminobutyric acid), Cortisol, Ghrelin, and Endocannabinoids

subject: Neurology
front: What does alpha-MSH stand for, and is it orexigenic or anorexigenic?
back: alpha-Melanocyte-stimulating hormone; it is anorexigenic (decreases feeding)

subject: Neurology
front: What does CRH stand for, and what is its effect on feeding?
back: Corticotropin-releasing hormone; it is anorexigenic (decreases feeding)

subject: Neurology
front: What does CART stand for, and what is its effect on feeding?
back: Cocaine & amphetamine regulated transcript; it is anorexigenic (decreases feeding)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the feeding effect of Peptide YY (PYY)?
back: Anorexigenic (decreases feeding)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the feeding effect of Neuropeptide Y (NPY)?
back: Orexigenic (increases feeding)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the feeding effect of Agouti-related protein (AGRP)?
back: Orexigenic (increases feeding)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the feeding effect of ghrelin and cortisol?
back: Both are orexigenic (increase feeding)

subject: Neurology
front: What hormone do adipocytes (fat cells) produce that suppresses appetite?
back: Leptin

subject: Neurology
front: What does adiponectin do, and which cells produce it?
back: Produced by adipocytes; it improves insulin sensitivity and is anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective

subject: Neurology
front: What input does the Ventral Posterolateral (VPL) thalamic nucleus receive, and what mnemonic helps recall its clinical relevance?
back: Receives input from the spinothalamic tract and dorsal column; mnemonic "Very painful limbs"

subject: Neurology
front: What sensations does the VPL nucleus relay, and to which cortical destination?
back: Somatic body sensations - pain, temperature, touch, vibration, and proprioception; relays to the primary somatosensory cortex (Brodmann Areas 3,1,2)

subject: Neurology
front: What input does the Ventral Posteromedial (VPM) thalamic nucleus receive, and what mnemonic helps recall its clinical relevance?
back: Receives input from the trigeminal nerve (sensory) and gustatory input via the Chorda Tympani, 9th cranial nerve, and 10th cranial nerve; mnemonic "Very painful mouth"

subject: Neurology
front: What sensations does the VPM nucleus relay, and to which cortical destinations?
back: Somatic face sensations and taste; somatic sensation goes to the primary somatosensory cortex (Brodmann Areas 3,1,2), while taste goes to Brodmann area 43

subject: Neurology
front: What input does the Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN) receive, and what mnemonic helps recall its function?
back: Receives input from the optic tract; mnemonic "L: Light (vision)"

subject: Neurology
front: To which cortical area does the LGN relay visual information, and what mnemonic marks this Brodmann area?
back: Primary visual cortex (Brodmann Area 17); mnemonic "we start seeing people at 17"

subject: Neurology
front: What input does the Medial Geniculate Nucleus (MGN) receive, and what mnemonic helps recall its function?
back: Receives input from the inferior colliculus of the auditory pathway; mnemonic "M: Music (hearing)"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the mnemonic for the auditory pathway structures leading into the MGN?
back: EECOLIMA

subject: Neurology
front: To which cortical area does the MGN relay auditory information?
back: Primary auditory cortex (Brodmann Areas 41/42)

subject: Neurology
front: What input do the Ventral Anterior/Ventral Lateral (VA/VL) thalamic nuclei receive, and what is their function?
back: Receive input from the basal ganglia and cerebellum; involved in motor planning and coordination

subject: Neurology
front: To which cortical area do the VA/VL nuclei relay information?
back: Motor cortex (Brodmann Areas 4,6)

subject: Neurology
front: Which thalamic nucleus is part of the limbic system?
back: The anterior nucleus

subject: Neurology
front: Which thalamic nucleus is involved in memory and is affected in Wernicke's encephalopathy?
back: The medial dorsal nucleus

subject: Neurology
front: Which thalamic nucleus is affected in Prion's disease?
back: The pulvinar

subject: Neurology
front: What is semantic memory, where is it processed, and what type of memory category does it belong to?
back: Memory of facts and general knowledge; retrieved via the prefrontal cortex, with storage involving the anterior and lateral temporal cortex; it is conscious and expressible, and belongs to declarative (explicit) memory

subject: Neurology
front: What is episodic memory, where is it located, and what happens with bilateral hippocampal lesions?
back: Memory of personal experiences and events, located in the hippocampus, neocortex, and medial temporal lobe (declarative/explicit memory); bilateral hippocampal lesions cause anterograde amnesia

subject: Neurology
front: What is procedural memory, where is it located, and what type of learning does it represent?
back: Memory for skills and habits, located in the striatum and cerebellum; represents unconscious learning (non-declarative/implicit memory)

subject: Neurology
front: What is priming/perceptual memory, and where is it located?
back: Improved response to a stimulus due to prior exposure (e.g., improved performance when given hints); located in the neocortex (non-declarative/implicit memory)

subject: Neurology
front: What is associative learning, what is a classic example, and where is it located?
back: Classical and operant conditioning (e.g., Pavlov's experiment); located in the amygdala and cerebellum (non-declarative/implicit memory)

subject: Neurology
front: What syndrome results from bilateral amygdala lesions, and what are its clinical features?
back: Kluver-Bucy Syndrome, characterized by hyperorality and hypersexuality

subject: Neurology
front: What is habituation, and what is its underlying cellular mechanism?
back: A decreased response to a repeated stimulus, caused by decreased calcium influx leading to decreased glutamate release

subject: Neurology
front: What is sensitization, and what is its underlying cellular mechanism?
back: An increased response to a stimulus, caused by increased serotonin leading to increased calcium influx and increased glutamate release

subject: Neurology
front: How long does short-term memory last?
back: 30 to 300 seconds

subject: Neurology
front: Where does long-term potentiation/depression occur, and which neurons are specifically involved?
back: In the hippocampus, involving CA1 neurons

subject: Neurology
front: Which neurotransmitter mediates long-term potentiation/depression?
back: Glutamate

subject: Neurology
front: Which two receptors mediate long-term potentiation, and what is the key characteristic of each?
back: The NMDA receptor (a slow channel) and the AMPA receptor (mediates fast excitatory transmission)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common cause of dementia, and what is the second most common cause?
back: Alzheimer's dementia is the most common cause; vascular dementia is the second most common, characterized by early executive dysfunction

subject: Neurology
front: What are the early clinical features of Alzheimer's dementia, and which brain lobes are involved early?
back: Short-term memory loss and spatial disorientation; the temporal and parietal lobes are involved early

subject: Neurology
front: What are the late clinical features of Alzheimer's dementia, and which brain lobe is involved late?
back: Personality changes; the frontal lobe is involved late

subject: Neurology
front: Which genetic condition is Alzheimer's disease associated with, and via which gene/chromosome?
back: Down's syndrome, via the APP gene located on chromosome 21

subject: Neurology
front: How do ApoE2 and ApoE4 alleles affect Alzheimer's disease prognosis?
back: ApoE2 is associated with a good prognosis; ApoE4 is associated with a bad prognosis

subject: Neurology
front: What is the treatment for mild Alzheimer's disease?
back: AChE inhibitors - Donepezil, Rivastigmine, and Galantamine

subject: Neurology
front: What is the treatment for moderate to severe Alzheimer's disease?
back: Memantine, an NMDA receptor inhibitor

subject: Neurology
front: Which monoclonal antibodies are used to treat Alzheimer's disease?
back: Lecanemab and Aducanumab

subject: Neurology
front: Which Alzheimer's disease drug is available as a transdermal patch?
back: Rivastigmine

subject: Neurology
front: What are neurofibrillary tangles composed of, and what is their histological appearance?
back: Composed of TAU proteins; appear as flame-shaped, basophilic structures (a non-specific finding)

subject: Neurology
front: What are Hirano bodies composed of, and what is their histological appearance?
back: Composed of actin; appear as eosinophilic, rod-like structures (a non-specific finding)

subject: Neurology
front: What does the Bielschowsky stain demonstrate, and what type of stain is it?
back: A silver stain that demonstrates beta-amyloid plaques

subject: Neurology
front: Which amyloid subtype is associated with Alzheimer's disease, and which is associated with cerebral amyloid angiopathy?
back: A-beta 42 is associated with Alzheimer's disease; A-beta 40 is associated with cerebral amyloid angiopathy, causing vascular fragility and lobar hemorrhages

subject: Neurology
front: What is the characteristic clinical course of vascular dementia?
back: A stepwise decline

subject: Neurology
front: What neuroimaging finding is seen in vascular dementia, and what is its cause?
back: Deep white matter changes, due to multiple infarcts or small vessel disease

subject: Neurology
front: What is Binswanger disease?
back: A subtype of vascular dementia caused by chronic small vessel ischemia

subject: Neurology
front: What are the early clinical features of Pick's disease (frontotemporal dementia)?
back: Early personality and behavioral changes

subject: Neurology
front: What gross pathological finding is characteristic of Pick's disease?
back: Knife-edge atrophy of the frontal lobe

subject: Neurology
front: What are "Pick bodies" composed of?
back: Tau proteins and ubiquitin

subject: Neurology
front: What is Hakim's triad of normal pressure hydrocephalus, and what mnemonic describes it?
back: Urinary incontinence, dementia, and gait ataxia; mnemonic "wet-wacky-wobbly"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the underlying mechanism causing symptoms in normal pressure hydrocephalus?
back: Cortical fibers of the frontal lobe become compressed/occluded due to lateral ventricular dilatation

subject: Neurology
front: What type of gait is characteristic of normal pressure hydrocephalus?
back: A shuffling gait with preserved arm swing, known as magnetic gait

subject: Neurology
front: Is normal pressure hydrocephalus reversible, and how is it treated?
back: Yes, it is a reversible dementia, treated with a VP (ventriculoperitoneal) shunt

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of Prion's disease?
back: Rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, infective proteins in CSF, periodic sharp waves on EEG, elevated 14-3-3 protein in CSF, and a history of neurosurgery/corneal transplant/cannibalism

subject: Neurology
front: What imaging sign is characteristic of variant CJD (a prion disease)?
back: The pulvinar sign, also called the hockey stick sign

subject: Neurology
front: What is the underlying pathology in Prion's disease, and is it transmissible?
back: Spongiform encephalopathy, which is transmissible

subject: Neurology
front: What causes HIV-Associated Neurocognitive Disorder (HAND), and what neuroimaging finding is seen?
back: Caused by HIV infection; causes diffuse (global) cerebral atrophy

subject: Neurology
front: What is the histological hallmark of HIV-Associated Neurocognitive Disorder?
back: Microglial nodules

subject: Neurology
front: What are the five main components of the basal ganglia?
back: Caudate, Putamen, Globus pallidus (GPi and GPe), Substantia nigra (SNpc and SNpr), and the Subthalamic nucleus

subject: Neurology
front: What are the two divisions of the globus pallidus?
back: Globus pallidus internal (GPi) and Globus pallidus external (GPe)

subject: Neurology
front: What are the two divisions of the substantia nigra?
back: SNpc (pars compacta) and SNpr (pars reticulata)

subject: Neurology
front: Which two structures together form the striatum?
back: Caudate + Putamen

subject: Neurology
front: Which two structures together form the lentiform nucleus?
back: Putamen + Globus pallidus

subject: Neurology
front: What is the overall functional role of the basal ganglia?
back: To inhibit unwanted/excess movements and facilitate smooth voluntary movement

subject: Neurology
front: What neurotransmitter mediates most basal ganglia output, and is it excitatory or inhibitory?
back: GABA, which is inhibitory

subject: Neurology
front: Which two basal ganglia structures are exceptions to the typical GABA/inhibitory output, and what neurotransmitters do they use instead?
back: The subthalamic nucleus (STN) uses glutamate; the SNpc uses dopamine

subject: Neurology
front: Damage to which basal ganglia structure causes chorea, and what are its features?
back: The caudate; chorea causes dance-like, purposeless movements

subject: Neurology
front: Damage to which basal ganglia structure causes athetosis, and what are its features?
back: The globus pallidus; athetosis causes slow, writhing movements

subject: Neurology
front: Damage to which basal ganglia structure causes parkinsonism, and what are its features?
back: The substantia nigra (SNpc); parkinsonism causes bradykinesia, rigidity, and tremor

subject: Neurology
front: Damage to which basal ganglia structure causes contralateral hemiballismus, and what are its features?
back: The subthalamic nucleus; hemiballismus causes violent flinging movements

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common movement disorder, and is the basal ganglia involved?
back: Essential tremor; the basal ganglia is NOT involved

subject: Neurology
front: What is the frequency and character of essential tremor?
back: A high-frequency tremor at 6-10 Hz, occurring as a postural/action tremor

subject: Neurology
front: What factors worsen essential tremor?
back: Activity and stress

subject: Neurology
front: What factor characteristically improves essential tremor?
back: Alcohol

subject: Neurology
front: Is essential tremor typically familial, and what is the drug of choice for treatment?
back: Yes, it is often familial; the drug of choice is propranolol

subject: Neurology
front: In the direct pathway of basal ganglia motor control, which dopamine receptor is activated, and what is its effect?
back: Dopamine acts on the D-1 receptor, producing an excitatory (+) effect

subject: Neurology
front: In the direct pathway, what does striatal activation do to the GPi, and what is the net effect on movement?
back: The striatum inhibits the GPi; since the GPi no longer inhibits the thalamus, the net effect is facilitation of movement ("double negative is positive")

subject: Neurology
front: In the indirect pathway of basal ganglia motor control, which dopamine receptor is activated, and what is its effect?
back: Dopamine acts on the D-2 receptor, producing an inhibitory (-) effect

subject: Neurology
front: In the indirect pathway, how does the striatum ultimately affect the GPi, and what is the net effect on movement?
back: Via a long path through the STN and GPe, the striatum excites the GPi; the GPi then inhibits the thalamus, resulting in no movement

subject: Neurology
front: What does the mnemonic TRAP stand for in defining parkinsonism?
back: Tremor, Rigidity, Akinesia/Bradykinesia, and Postural instability

subject: Neurology
front: What is another name for parkinsonism?
back: Paralysis Agitans

subject: Neurology
front: Describe the classic tremor of parkinsonism, including its frequency.
back: A "pill-rolling" resting tremor occurring at 3-6 Hz

subject: Neurology
front: Describe the type of rigidity seen in parkinsonism.
back: "Lead-pipe" or "cogwheel" stiffness

subject: Neurology
front: What does akinesia/bradykinesia refer to in parkinsonism?
back: Slowness or lack of movement

subject: Neurology
front: What does postural instability refer to in parkinsonism?
back: Balance problems and falls

subject: Neurology
front: What is micrographia, as seen in parkinsonism?
back: Small, cramped handwriting

subject: Neurology
front: What is meant by "mask-like facies" in parkinsonism?
back: Reduced facial expression

subject: Neurology
front: Describe the shuffling gait characteristic of parkinsonism.
back: Short steps with reduced arm swing

subject: Neurology
front: What are the late clinical features of parkinsonism?
back: Dementia and depression

subject: Neurology
front: How does classic parkinsonism (Parkinson's disease) respond to dopamine, and how does this differ from Parkinson-plus syndromes?
back: Parkinson's disease has a good response to dopamine, whereas Parkinson-plus syndromes have a poor response

subject: Neurology
front: What is meant by "fluctuating cognition" in Lewy body dementia?
back: The patient may be lucid at one time and confused shortly afterward

subject: Neurology
front: What is a characteristic early clinical sign of Lewy body dementia?
back: Visual hallucinations

subject: Neurology
front: What is REM Sleep Behavior Disorder (RBD), as seen in Lewy body dementia?
back: Loss of normal REM sleep paralysis, causing patients to act out their dreams (kicking, shouting, movements)

subject: Neurology
front: How prominent is parkinsonism in Lewy body dementia?
back: Spontaneous parkinsonism occurs with bradykinesia and rigidity, but tremor is less prominent

subject: Neurology
front: What protein forms Lewy bodies?
back: Alpha-synuclein

subject: Neurology
front: List the three synucleinopathies mentioned in the notes, with their key features.
back: Parkinson's disease (most common); Dementia with Lewy bodies (early dementia + visual hallucinations + Parkinsonism features); Multiple system atrophy (Parkinsonism + autonomic failure, e.g. hypotension, urinary issues)

subject: Neurology
front: List the seven tauopathies mentioned in the notes.
back: Progressive supranuclear palsy, Corticobasal degeneration, Alzheimer's disease, Pick's disease, Chronic traumatic encephalopathy, Pantothenate kinase-associated degeneration, and Subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (a measles complication)

subject: Neurology
front: What two general features characterize the "Parkinson-plus" syndromes as a group?
back: Poor response to dopamine and absence of tremors

subject: Neurology
front: What are the key features of Multiple System Atrophy (MSA-C)?
back: Autonomic features and the "hot cross bun" sign in the pons

subject: Neurology
front: What are the key features of Lewy body dementia listed among the Parkinson-plus category?
back: Dementia and visual hallucinations

subject: Neurology
front: What are the key features of Progressive Supranuclear Palsy?
back: Impaired downward gaze and the "hummingbird sign"

subject: Neurology
front: What are the key features of Corticobasal Degeneration?
back: Alien limb phenomenon and asymmetric bradykinesia

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for Parkinson's disease in elderly patients (age 65 or older)?
back: Levodopa + Carbidopa (Syndopa)

subject: Neurology
front: Which Parkinson's disease symptoms does Levodopa + Carbidopa treat most effectively?
back: Bradykinesia and rigidity

subject: Neurology
front: Why is Levodopa + Carbidopa not preferred as first-line in young Parkinson's disease patients?
back: Due to side effects of motor fluctuations and dyskinesia with long-term use

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for Parkinson's disease in young patients?
back: Dopamine agonists - Pramipexole, Ropinirole

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for drug-induced parkinsonism or tremor-predominant Parkinson's disease?
back: Central anticholinergics - Trihexyphenidyl (also known as Benztropine)

subject: Neurology
front: What is Istradefylline, and what class of drug is it?
back: A newer Parkinson's disease drug that acts as an adenosine (A2A) receptor antagonist

subject: Neurology
front: What is the management approach for advanced Parkinson's disease, and what are its targets?
back: Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS), targeting the subthalamic nucleus (STN) and Globus pallidus interna (GPi)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the "on-off phenomenon" seen with Syndopa (Levodopa + Carbidopa) therapy?
back: "On" periods feature dyskinesia, while "off" periods feature akinesia and tremors

subject: Neurology
front: How is the on-off phenomenon of Syndopa therapy managed?
back: With COMT inhibitors or MAO-B inhibitors

subject: Neurology
front: Which class of MAO inhibitors is used in depression, as opposed to Parkinson's disease?
back: MAO-A inhibitors

subject: Neurology
front: What is levodopa-induced dyskinesia (LID), and when does it occur?
back: Dyskinesia that occurs during the "on" period of levodopa therapy

subject: Neurology
front: How is levodopa-induced dyskinesia treated, and what are the side effects of this treatment?
back: Treated with Amantadine; side effects include edema and livedo reticularis

subject: Neurology
front: What are the adverse effects associated with dopamine agonist therapy in Parkinson's disease?
back: Impulse control disorders - pathological gambling, hypersexuality, and compulsive shopping

subject: Neurology
front: Why is bromocriptine avoided as a dopamine agonist in Parkinson's disease?
back: Due to side effects of vasoconstriction and cardiac valvular fibrosis

subject: Neurology
front: Which dopamine agonist for Parkinson's disease is available as a transdermal patch?
back: Rotigotine

subject: Neurology
front: What enzyme does carbidopa inhibit, and why is it co-administered with levodopa?
back: Carbidopa inhibits DOPA decarboxylase, preventing peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine so that more levodopa reaches the brain

subject: Neurology
front: Name two peripheral COMT inhibitors used in Parkinson's disease.
back: Entacapone and Tolcapone

subject: Neurology
front: Which COMT inhibitor acts centrally as well as peripherally?
back: Tolcapone

subject: Neurology
front: Name two MAO type-B inhibitors used in Parkinson's disease.
back: Selegiline and Rasagiline

subject: Neurology
front: Which drug increases dopamine availability and is also used to treat levodopa-induced dyskinesia?
back: Amantadine

subject: Neurology
front: Name the non-ergot dopamine agonists used in Parkinson's disease.
back: Pramipexole and Ropinirole

subject: Neurology
front: Name the ergot dopamine agonist used in Parkinson's disease (generally avoided).
back: Bromocriptine

subject: Neurology
front: What clinical features result from lesions of the vermis, flocculonodular lobe, or anterior lobe of the cerebellum?
back: Truncal ataxia, staggering gait, and nystagmus; classically seen in alcoholics

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the flocculonodular lobe (vestibulocerebellum)?
back: Maintains balance

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the anterior lobe (spinocerebellum)?
back: Coordination

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the dentate nucleus (lateral hemisphere/cerebro-cerebellum)?
back: Motor planning

subject: Neurology
front: What clinical features result from lesions of the dentate nucleus (lateral cerebellar hemisphere)?
back: Dysmetria, intention tremor, and dysdiadochokinesia

subject: Neurology
front: What additional reflex finding can be seen with cerebellar dysfunction?
back: Pendular knee jerk

subject: Neurology
front: What is the overall function of the vermis, flocculonodular lobe, and anterior lobe together?
back: They maintain truncal balance

subject: Neurology
front: What is the overall function of the lateral hemisphere/cerebro-cerebellum?
back: Fine movement of the limbs

subject: Neurology
front: List the four deep cerebellar nuclei from lateral to medial, with their mnemonic.
back: Dentate, Emboliform, Globose, Fastigial (mnemonic: "dentate, emboliform, globose, fastigial")

subject: Neurology
front: Through which deep cerebellar nucleus, tract, and peduncle do most cerebellar efferents pass?
back: Most efferents pass via the dentate nucleus, through the dentato-rubro-thalamic tract, via the superior cerebellar peduncle

subject: Neurology
front: Which deep cerebellar nucleus is phylogenetically the oldest?
back: The fastigial nucleus

subject: Neurology
front: What tracts pass through the superior cerebellar peduncle?
back: The dentato-thalamic and dentato-rubro-thalamic tracts, and the ventral spinocerebellar tract

subject: Neurology
front: What is notable about the middle cerebellar peduncle, and what does it carry?
back: It is the largest cerebellar peduncle and is purely afferent, carrying the cortico-ponto-cerebellar tract

subject: Neurology
front: What does the inferior cerebellar peduncle carry?
back: Everything else - the remaining afferent and efferent tracts not carried by the superior or middle peduncles

subject: Neurology
front: What is unique about the cerebellar cortex in terms of neurotransmission?
back: It is the largest collection of inhibitory fibers in the brain

subject: Neurology
front: What mnemonic describes the excitatory relationship between granular cells and mossy fibers in the cerebellar cortex?
back: "Granny (granular cells) is excited to meet mausi (mossy fibers)"

subject: Neurology
front: Which afferent fibers to the cerebellum are climbing fibers, and what mnemonic helps recall this?
back: Olivocerebellar and paraolivocerebellar tract fibers; mnemonic "olives (like grapes) are climbers"

subject: Neurology
front: What are the remaining (non-climbing) afferent fibers to the cerebellum called?
back: Mossy fibers (mnemonic: "Mausi will only go to excited granny")

subject: Neurology
front: What is the final efferent pathway out of the cerebellar cortex?
back: Purkinje cells project to the deep cerebellar nuclei

subject: Neurology
front: What headache characteristics (quality, aura, duration) suggest migraine?
back: Pulsatile quality, aura may be present, and duration of 4-72 hours

subject: Neurology
front: What group of unilateral headaches feature recurrent attacks with ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms, and what are those symptoms?
back: Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias; symptoms include lacrimation, conjunctival injection, periorbital pain, and Horner syndrome

subject: Neurology
front: What is the typical duration and demographic pattern of cluster headache?
back: 15 minutes to 3 hours in duration; more common in males than females

subject: Neurology
front: What is the acute treatment for cluster headache?
back: 100% high-flow oxygen (12-15 L/min); triptans can also be used

subject: Neurology
front: What is the prophylactic treatment for cluster headache?
back: Calcium channel blockers (CCBs)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the typical duration of paroxysmal hemicrania, and what is its diagnostic clue?
back: 2-30 minutes; shows a dramatic response to indomethacin

subject: Neurology
front: What does SUNCT stand for, and what is its typical duration and pain quality?
back: Short-lasting Unilateral Neuralgiform headache attacks with Conjunctival injection and Tearing; lasts 5-200 seconds with burning, stabbing pain

subject: Neurology
front: What is the typical duration, location, and trigger of trigeminal neuralgia?
back: 5-200 seconds, in the V2/V3 trigeminal nerve territory, triggered by chewing or touch

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for trigeminal neuralgia, and what is done in refractory cases?
back: Carbamazepine is the drug of choice; refractory cases may require gamma knife surgery

subject: Neurology
front: What are the characteristics of tension-type headache?
back: A band-like sensation lasting 4-6 hours

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common type of headache overall?
back: Tension-type headache

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for acute tension-type headache, and what is used for prophylaxis?
back: NSAIDs for acute treatment; TCAs (tricyclic antidepressants) for prophylaxis when headaches occur more than 15 days a month

subject: Neurology
front: What are the characteristic features of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), and its other name?
back: Dull, aching headache with papilledema; also known as pseudotumor cerebri

subject: Neurology
front: What cranial nerve palsy is characteristic of IIH, and what symptom results?
back: 6th cranial nerve palsy, causing diplopia

subject: Neurology
front: What is the CSF finding in idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
back: Raised intracranial pressure with normal CSF composition

subject: Neurology
front: What is the first-line treatment for idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
back: Weight loss and acetazolamide

subject: Neurology
front: What treatments are used for refractory IIH or when vision is threatened?
back: Repeated lumbar punctures or optic nerve sheath fenestration

subject: Neurology
front: What characteristic imaging finding is seen in idiopathic intracranial hypertension?
back: Optic nerve sheath dilation

subject: Neurology
front: What are the risk factors for idiopathic intracranial hypertension, and what mnemonic covers them?
back: Tetracycline, Obesity, Vitamin A, and Danazol; mnemonic "TOAD"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the first-line acute treatment for migraine?
back: NSAIDs

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for acute migraine treatment, and what is its mechanism of action?
back: Triptans, which are 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonists

subject: Neurology
front: What are the contraindications to triptan use, and why?
back: CAD, stroke, and hypertension, due to the risk of vasoconstriction

subject: Neurology
front: What drug is used for acute migraine when triptans are contraindicated, and why is it safer?
back: Lasmiditan, a selective 5-HT1F agonist, which does not cause vasoconstriction and is safe in CAD/stroke

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for migraine prophylaxis?
back: Propranolol, a beta-blocker

subject: Neurology
front: What other drugs are first-line options for migraine prophylaxis besides propranolol?
back: Topiramate and Valproate

subject: Neurology
front: What are gepants, name two examples, and what are they used for in migraine?
back: CGRP antagonists; examples are Rimegepant and Atogepant; useful for acute migraine treatment and also for prophylaxis

subject: Neurology
front: Name four monoclonal antibodies used in migraine treatment.
back: Erenumab, Galcanezumab, Fremanezumab, and Eptinezumab

subject: Neurology
front: What are the normal CSF values for WBC count, glucose, protein, and opening pressure?
back: WBC 0-5 cells/uL, glucose 40-70 mg/dL, protein less than 40 mg/dL, opening pressure 50-180 mm H2O; CSF is normally clear

subject: Neurology
front: What are the characteristic CSF findings in bacterial meningitis?
back: Turbid CSF with markedly increased WBC count (predominantly neutrophils), markedly decreased glucose, and markedly increased protein and opening pressure

subject: Neurology
front: What are the characteristic CSF findings in tubercular meningitis, and what is its classic gross appearance?
back: Turbid CSF, classically forming a "cobweb coagulum" on standing, with markedly increased WBC count (predominantly lymphocytes), markedly decreased glucose, and markedly increased protein and opening pressure

subject: Neurology
front: What are the characteristic CSF findings in viral meningitis (the more common type)?
back: Clear CSF with mildly increased lymphocyte count, normal or slightly decreased glucose, mildly increased protein, and normal opening pressure

subject: Neurology
front: What are the characteristic CSF findings in fungal meningitis, and what is a key risk factor?
back: Clear CSF with mildly increased lymphocyte count, normal or slightly decreased glucose, mildly increased protein, and normal opening pressure; associated with a history of HIV

subject: Neurology
front: What are the characteristic CSF findings in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
back: Clear CSF with normal WBC count and normal glucose, but markedly increased protein (called albuminocytologic dissociation), with normal opening pressure

subject: Neurology
front: What CSF finding is characteristic of multiple sclerosis?
back: Oligoclonal bands

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common form of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
back: AIDP (Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Polyneuropathy)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the classic clinical presentation of Guillain-Barré syndrome?
back: Bilateral acute ascending flaccid paralysis with areflexia

subject: Neurology
front: What antecedent infection is classically associated with Guillain-Barré syndrome?
back: A prior gastrointestinal infection, especially with Campylobacter jejuni

subject: Neurology
front: What is the typical time course of Guillain-Barré syndrome, and what happens if symptoms exceed this duration or relapse?
back: It follows a monophasic course lasting less than 4 weeks; if it lasts more than 8 weeks, or relapses 3 or more times, it is reclassified as CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy)

subject: Neurology
front: What diagnostic criteria are used to diagnose Guillain-Barré syndrome?
back: The Brighton criteria

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common cause of death in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
back: Respiratory paralysis

subject: Neurology
front: What is the "20-30-40 rule" for monitoring respiratory function in Guillain-Barré syndrome?
back: Vital capacity less than 20 mL/kg, Maximum Inspiratory Pressure (MIP) less than -30 cm H2O, and Maximum Expiratory Pressure (MEP) less than 40 cm H2O - these indicate the need for elective intubation

subject: Neurology
front: What is the treatment for Guillain-Barré syndrome, and which treatment is notably NOT used?
back: IVIG or plasmapheresis; steroids are NOT used

subject: Neurology
front: Which antibody is associated with the acute demyelinating (AIDP) variant of GBS?
back: Anti-GM1 antibodies

subject: Neurology
front: What are the axonal variants of GBS, and which antibody is associated with AMAN?
back: AMAN (Acute Motor Axonal Neuropathy, associated with anti-GD1A antibodies) and AMSAN (Acute Motor and Sensory Axonal Neuropathy)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the ophthalmoplegic variant of GBS called, and which antibody is associated with it?
back: Miller Fisher syndrome, associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies

subject: Neurology
front: What are the nerve conduction velocity (NCV) findings in demyelinating neuropathy?
back: Decreased conduction velocity and prolonged latencies

subject: Neurology
front: What are the NCV findings in axonal variant neuropathy?
back: Decreased amplitude, with preserved conduction velocity

subject: Neurology
front: What is Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, and how common is it?
back: The most common inherited peripheral neuropathy

subject: Neurology
front: What genetic abnormality causes Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, and what is the resulting mechanism?
back: Duplication of the PMP22 gene, causing demyelination

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease?
back: Distal muscle weakness and atrophy, areflexia (due to peripheral nerve involvement), foot drop, and pes cavus (hammer toes)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the characteristic nerve biopsy finding in Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease?
back: Onion bulb appearance

subject: Neurology
front: What is the characteristic clinical sign of distal leg muscle wasting in Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease?
back: The inverted champagne bottle sign

subject: Neurology
front: How do myelitis segment length and optic neuritis laterality differ between multiple sclerosis and ADEM/NMO?
back: MS shows short segment myelitis (less than 3 vertebral bodies) with unilateral optic neuritis; ADEM and NMO show long segment myelitis (more than 3 vertebral bodies) with bilateral optic neuritis

subject: Neurology
front: What age group and sex predominance is typical of multiple sclerosis?
back: 20-40 years, with female predominance

subject: Neurology
front: What geographic and vitamin association is seen with multiple sclerosis?
back: It is more common away from the equator, and is associated with low vitamin D levels

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common clinical subtype of multiple sclerosis?
back: Relapsing remitting type (RRMS)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the investigation of choice for multiple sclerosis, and what criteria are used?
back: Contrast-enhanced (CE) MRI, applying McDonald's criteria

subject: Neurology
front: What does the McDonald criteria for multiple sclerosis diagnosis require?
back: Dissemination in time and space

subject: Neurology
front: What are the three components of the Charcot triad in multiple sclerosis?
back: Scanning speech, intentional tremors, and nystagmus

subject: Neurology
front: What is the Lhermitte sign, and what mnemonic describes it?
back: A shock-like sensation on flexion of the neck; mnemonic "Lower-my-neck"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the Uhthoff phenomenon in multiple sclerosis?
back: Exacerbation of symptoms with heat exposure (e.g., a hot shower)

subject: Neurology
front: What MRI findings are characteristic of multiple sclerosis?
back: Dawson fingers and periventricular white matter involvement

subject: Neurology
front: What is the treatment for an acute relapse of multiple sclerosis?
back: Steroids

subject: Neurology
front: What drugs are used for chronic management of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS)?
back: Beta-interferons, Natalizumab, Mitoxantrone, Fingolimod, and Alemtuzumab

subject: Neurology
front: What serious side effect is associated with Natalizumab, and what is its mechanism of action?
back: It blocks alpha4beta1 integrin, and carries the serious side effect risk of PML (progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy)

subject: Neurology
front: Which drug is the only one approved for primary progressive multiple sclerosis (PPMS), and in which trial was it studied?
back: Ocrelizumab, studied in the ORATORIO trial

subject: Neurology
front: What age group is typically affected by ADEM (Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis)?
back: Children, under 20 years of age

subject: Neurology
front: What preceding event is classically associated with ADEM?
back: A history of antecedent viral infection

subject: Neurology
front: What is the typical clinical course of ADEM?
back: Monophasic - it does not recur

subject: Neurology
front: What characteristic MRI finding is seen in ADEM?
back: Multiple fluffy, cotton-wool lesions

subject: Neurology
front: What age group is typically affected by Neuromyelitis Optica (Devic's disease)?
back: 20-40 years

subject: Neurology
front: What antibody is associated with Neuromyelitis Optica?
back: Anti-aquaporin 4 antibodies

subject: Neurology
front: What four clinical syndromes are seen in Neuromyelitis Optica, reflecting peri-ventricular involvement?
back: Area postrema syndrome, diencephalon syndrome, acute myelitis, and bilateral optic neuritis

subject: Neurology
front: What virus causes Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML), and which cell type does it infect?
back: JC virus; it infects oligodendrocytes

subject: Neurology
front: What type of infection is PML, and what imaging pattern does it typically show?
back: An opportunistic infection, showing asymmetric white matter involvement

subject: Neurology
front: In which patient populations is PML seen?
back: Patients with HIV/AIDS and patients on Natalizumab therapy

subject: Neurology
front: What sensations does the anterior spinothalamic tract carry?
back: Crude touch, pressure, and itch

subject: Neurology
front: What sensations does the lateral spinothalamic tract carry?
back: Pain and temperature

subject: Neurology
front: At what level do spinothalamic tract fibers cross to the opposite side?
back: At the level of the spinal cord

subject: Neurology
front: What sensations does the dorsal column-medial lemniscus pathway carry?
back: Fine touch, vibration, conscious proprioception, and two-point discrimination

subject: Neurology
front: At what level do dorsal column fibers cross to the opposite side?
back: At the level of the medulla

subject: Neurology
front: Which clinical sign is positive when the dorsal column pathway is defective?
back: Romberg sign

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the corticospinal tract?
back: Voluntary motor control

subject: Neurology
front: What is the function of the corticobulbar tract?
back: Voluntary motor control of the face, head, and neck

subject: Neurology
front: In the dorsal column, which fibers are located medially and which laterally, and what mnemonic helps recall this?
back: Sacral fibers (fasciculus gracilis) lie medially; cervical fibers (fasciculus cuneatus) lie laterally; mnemonic "Gracious legs"

subject: Neurology
front: In the spinothalamic and corticospinal tracts, which fibers are located medially and which laterally?
back: Cervical fibers lie medially; sacral fibers lie laterally (the opposite arrangement compared to the dorsal column)

subject: Neurology
front: What body region does the fasciculus gracilis carry sensation from?
back: The lower body/legs

subject: Neurology
front: What body region does the fasciculus cuneatus carry sensation from?
back: The upper body/arms

subject: Neurology
front: Where is the intermediate horn (containing sympathetic neurons) located in the spinal cord?
back: From T1 to L2/L3

subject: Neurology
front: Which cranial nerves are involved in a pseudobulbar (supranuclear/UMN) palsy?
back: Cranial nerves 5, 7, 10, 11, and 12

subject: Neurology
front: Which cranial nerves are involved in a bulbar (LMN) palsy?
back: Cranial nerves 9, 10, 11, and 12

subject: Neurology
front: How does the gag reflex differ between pseudobulbar and bulbar palsy?
back: Exaggerated in pseudobulbar (UMN) palsy; absent in bulbar (LMN) palsy

subject: Neurology
front: How does the jaw jerk differ between pseudobulbar and bulbar palsy?
back: Exaggerated in pseudobulbar (UMN) palsy; absent in bulbar (LMN) palsy

subject: Neurology
front: How does tongue involvement differ between pseudobulbar and bulbar palsy?
back: Spasticity occurs in pseudobulbar (UMN) palsy; fasciculations occur in bulbar (LMN) palsy

subject: Neurology
front: How does speech differ between pseudobulbar and bulbar palsy?
back: Laboured/spastic speech occurs in pseudobulbar palsy; nasal twang with nasal regurgitation occurs in bulbar palsy

subject: Neurology
front: How does weakness compare between UMN and LMN lesions?
back: Weakness is present in both UMN and LMN lesions

subject: Neurology
front: How do reflexes compare between UMN and LMN lesions?
back: Reflexes are increased in UMN lesions and decreased in LMN lesions

subject: Neurology
front: How does muscle tone compare between UMN and LMN lesions?
back: Tone is spastic in UMN lesions and flaccid in LMN lesions

subject: Neurology
front: How does the Babinski sign compare between UMN and LMN lesions?
back: Babinski sign is positive in UMN lesions and negative in LMN lesions

subject: Neurology
front: How do atrophy and fasciculations compare between UMN and LMN lesions?
back: Atrophy and fasciculations are absent in UMN lesions and present in LMN lesions

subject: Neurology
front: What does a positive Babinski sign (extensor plantar response) indicate?
back: An upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion

subject: Neurology
front: What is the method for eliciting the Oppenheim sign (a Babinski response variant)?
back: Firmly stroking down the medial tibia

subject: Neurology
front: What is the method for eliciting the Chaddock sign (a Babinski response variant)?
back: Stroking around the lateral malleolus

subject: Neurology
front: What is the method for eliciting the Gordon sign (a Babinski response variant)?
back: Squeezing the calf muscles

subject: Neurology
front: What is preserved in an anterior spinal artery infarct, and why?
back: The dorsal columns (fine touch and proprioception) are preserved, since the anterior spinal artery does not supply them

subject: Neurology
front: In what clinical setting is anterior spinal artery infarct classically seen?
back: Post aortic procedures, such as EVAR (endovascular aneurysm repair)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the underlying cause of tabes dorsalis?
back: Tertiary syphilis

subject: Neurology
front: What clinical features are seen in tabes dorsalis?
back: Lancinating pain, positive Romberg sign, and Argyll Robertson pupil (with the accommodation reflex present)

subject: Neurology
front: Which spinal cord tracts are involved in tabes dorsalis?
back: Both dorsal columns

subject: Neurology
front: What is syringomyelia, and what is its characteristic distribution of sensory loss?
back: Dilation of the central canal of the spinal cord, causing a cape-like distribution of sensory loss over the shoulders and arms

subject: Neurology
front: Which tract is always spared in syringomyelia?
back: The dorsal column

subject: Neurology
front: What congenital malformation is syringomyelia associated with?
back: Chiari I malformation

subject: Neurology
front: What are the three causes of subacute combined degeneration (SACD)?
back: Vitamin B12 deficiency, ileal disease/resection, and nitrous oxide (N2O) exposure (e.g., from old operating theater use)

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of subacute combined degeneration (SACD)?
back: Bilateral UMN signs plus dorsal column involvement

subject: Neurology
front: What is the underlying pathology of poliomyelitis, and what is the pattern of destruction?
back: Asymmetric destruction of anterior horn cells

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of poliomyelitis?
back: A viral prodrome (e.g., diarrhea) followed by bilateral flaccid (LMN) paralysis

subject: Neurology
front: What is the underlying genetic cause of spinal muscular atrophy (Werdnig-Hoffmann disease)?
back: A defect in the SMN gene

subject: Neurology
front: What is the clinical presentation of spinal muscular atrophy, and what type of motor neuron disorder is it?
back: A "floppy baby" presentation, with symmetric anterior horn cell loss; it is a pure LMN disorder

subject: Neurology
front: What treatments are available for spinal muscular atrophy?
back: Nusinersen, Onasemnogene (Zolgensma), and Risdiplam

subject: Neurology
front: What is another name for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)?
back: Lou Gehrig's disease

subject: Neurology
front: What clinical features characterize ALS, and what is notably spared?
back: Both UMN and LMN signs are present; sensory loss, bowel/bladder involvement, and cognitive decline are notably absent

subject: Neurology
front: What pathological findings are characteristic of ALS?
back: Bunina bodies and TDP-43 inclusions

subject: Neurology
front: What treatments are available for ALS?
back: Riluzole, Edaravone, Sodium phenylbutyrate-taurursodiol, and Tofersen

subject: Neurology
front: What sensory deficit occurs contralateral to a Brown-Séquard (hemicord) lesion, and why?
back: Loss of pain and temperature sensation, because the spinothalamic tract (already crossed) is affected contralaterally

subject: Neurology
front: What findings occur ipsilateral to and below the level of a Brown-Séquard lesion?
back: UMN signs

subject: Neurology
front: What findings occur ipsilateral to and at the level of a Brown-Séquard lesion?
back: LMN signs and loss of all sensations

subject: Neurology
front: What is the genetic basis of Friedreich ataxia?
back: A GAA repeat expansion on chromosome 9, inherited in an autosomal recessive (AR) pattern

subject: Neurology
front: Which tracts are involved in Friedreich ataxia?
back: The spinocerebellar tracts

subject: Neurology
front: What nutritional deficiency can Friedreich ataxia clinically mimic?
back: Vitamin E deficiency

subject: Neurology
front: What systemic features are associated with Friedreich ataxia?
back: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, diabetes mellitus, and kyphoscoliosis

subject: Neurology
front: What is the classic weakness pattern seen in myasthenia gravis?
back: Ptosis and diplopia starting with the eyelids, worsening in the evening, and improving with rest

subject: Neurology
front: What functions are notably NOT involved in myasthenia gravis?
back: Sensory function, autonomic function, deep tendon reflexes (DTRs), bowel/bladder function, and pupillary function

subject: Neurology
front: What bedside test can support a diagnosis of myasthenia gravis, and what is the expected finding?
back: The ice-pack test; it shows improvement of symptoms with cooling

subject: Neurology
front: What are the two clinical types of myasthenia gravis?
back: Ocular and generalized

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common antibody found in myasthenia gravis?
back: Anti-acetylcholine receptor (anti-AChR) antibodies

subject: Neurology
front: If anti-AChR antibodies are negative in myasthenia gravis, which antibody should be tested, and what is its frequency in generalized MG?
back: Anti-MuSK antibody; found in 40% of seronegative cases of generalized myasthenia gravis

subject: Neurology
front: What is the drug of choice for treating myasthenia gravis?
back: Pyridostigmine

subject: Neurology
front: How is myasthenic crisis managed?
back: With IVIG or plasmapheresis

subject: Neurology
front: What is a newer drug used in the treatment of myasthenia gravis?
back: Rozanolixizumab

subject: Neurology
front: Which classes of drugs should be avoided in patients with myasthenia gravis?
back: Beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers (CCBs), fluoroquinolones, beta-lactams, and aminoglycosides

subject: Neurology
front: What is a key differential diagnosis for myasthenia gravis, and what are its clinical features?
back: Critical illness myopathy/neuropathy, featuring proximal weakness and axonal degeneration

subject: Neurology
front: Where is the site of the pathological defect in myasthenia gravis?
back: Postsynaptic, at the acetylcholine receptors

subject: Neurology
front: Where is the site of the pathological defect in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS)?
back: Presynaptic, at the voltage-gated calcium channels

subject: Neurology
front: What antibodies are typically found in myasthenia gravis?
back: Anti-AChR and anti-MuSK antibodies

subject: Neurology
front: What antibodies are typically found in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome?
back: Antibodies against voltage-gated calcium channels

subject: Neurology
front: What is the repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) response pattern in myasthenia gravis?
back: A decremental response

subject: Neurology
front: What is the repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) response pattern in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome?
back: An incremental response

subject: Neurology
front: What tumor is myasthenia gravis classically associated with, and what workup follows?
back: Thymoma; the thymus should be evaluated

subject: Neurology
front: What malignancy is Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome classically associated with?
back: Small cell lung carcinoma

subject: Neurology
front: How does weakness behave with repeated muscle use in myasthenia gravis versus LEMS?
back: In myasthenia gravis, weakness worsens with use; in LEMS, weakness improves with use

subject: Neurology
front: Which cerebral hemisphere controls speech/language, and in whom is this typical?
back: The dominant hemisphere, usually the left hemisphere in right-handed individuals

subject: Neurology
front: What are the three brain areas responsible for speech/language function?
back: Broca's area, Wernicke's area, and the arcuate fasciculus (which connects the two)

subject: Neurology
front: What Brodmann area, gyrus, and MCA branch correspond to Broca's area?
back: Brodmann area 44, located in the inferior frontal gyrus, supplied by the superior branch of the MCA

subject: Neurology
front: What is the characteristic speech pattern in Broca's aphasia?
back: "Broken speech", but comprehension is preserved

subject: Neurology
front: What Brodmann area, gyrus, and MCA branch correspond to Wernicke's area?
back: Brodmann area 22, located in the superior temporal gyrus, supplied by the inferior branch of the MCA

subject: Neurology
front: What is the characteristic speech pattern in Wernicke's aphasia?
back: Fluent but non-comprehensible ("wacky") speech, with impaired understanding

subject: Neurology
front: What is conduction aphasia, and which structure is involved?
back: An aphasia in which the patient cannot repeat words, due to involvement of the arcuate fasciculus

subject: Neurology
front: What happens to repetition ability when Broca's area, Wernicke's area, or the arcuate fasciculus is damaged?
back: Repetition becomes impaired

subject: Neurology
front: What are transcortical aphasias, and how do they differ from Broca's/Wernicke's/conduction aphasia?
back: Aphasias in which repetition is preserved, unlike the other three types where repetition is impaired

subject: Neurology
front: What defines transcortical motor aphasia?
back: Reduced fluency with intact repetition

subject: Neurology
front: What defines transcortical sensory aphasia?
back: Reduced comprehension with intact repetition

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann area corresponds to the primary motor cortex?
back: Area 4

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann area corresponds to the frontal eye field, and what is its function?
back: Area 8; it controls voluntary conjugate gaze (saccades)

subject: Neurology
front: In which direction do the eyes deviate with a frontal eye field (FEF) lesion?
back: Towards the side of the lesion

subject: Neurology
front: In which direction do the eyes deviate with a PPRF (paramedian pontine reticular formation) lesion?
back: Away from the side of the lesion

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann areas correspond to the primary somatosensory cortex?
back: Areas 3, 1, and 2

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann area corresponds to the vestibular cortex?
back: Area 2

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann areas correspond to the somatosensory association cortex?
back: Areas 5, 7, and 40

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann areas correspond to the visual association cortex, and what syndrome results from a dominant hemisphere lesion there?
back: Areas 39, 19, and 18; a dominant hemisphere lesion causes Gerstmann syndrome

subject: Neurology
front: What is the classic tetrad of Gerstmann syndrome?
back: Agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and right-left disorientation

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann area corresponds to the primary visual cortex, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: Area 17; mnemonic "we start seeing people at 17"

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann areas correspond to the prefrontal cortex?
back: Areas 9, 10, 11, and 12

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann areas correspond to Broca's area?
back: Areas 44 and 45

subject: Neurology
front: Which Brodmann areas correspond to the primary auditory cortex, and what is another name for this region?
back: Areas 41 and 42; also known as Heschl's gyrus

subject: Neurology
front: State the Law of Projection regarding sensation.
back: Sensation is always perceived at the site of the receptor, regardless of where along the sensory pathway the stimulus actually occurs

subject: Neurology
front: Give a clinical example illustrating the Law of Projection.
back: After amputation, stimulating the remaining nerve stump causes the patient to feel pain in the missing limb

subject: Neurology
front: What is cortical plasticity (S1 reorganization)?
back: The reorganization of the primary somatosensory cortex that occurs after a loss or change in sensory input

subject: Neurology
front: Give an example of cortical plasticity after hand amputation.
back: The face representation area expands into the area that previously represented the hand, so touching the face produces sensation felt in the phantom hand

subject: Neurology
front: What two mechanisms combine to produce phantom limb pain?
back: The Law of Projection (pain felt in the missing limb) combined with cortical plasticity (abnormal remapping that sustains the sensation)

subject: Neurology
front: How is stroke defined, and what two categories does it include?
back: An acute focal neurological deficit of vascular origin, including ischemic stroke and hemorrhagic stroke

subject: Neurology
front: What does the FAST mnemonic stand for in rapid stroke recognition?
back: Face (one-sided drooping), Arm (weakness), Speech (slurred/aphasia), Time (call emergency immediately) - "Time is brain"

subject: Neurology
front: What is a TIA (Transient Ischemic Attack)?
back: A brief, reversible neurological deficit with no infarction seen on MRI (normal DWI)

subject: Neurology
front: What score is used to assess the risk of future stroke after a TIA?
back: The ABCD2 score

subject: Neurology
front: What are the components and point values of the ABCD2 score?
back: Age >=60 years (1 point); BP >=140/90 mmHg (1 point); speech disturbance only (1 point) or unilateral weakness (2 points); symptom duration 10-59 minutes (1 point) or >=60 minutes (2 points); diabetes mellitus (1 point)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the initial imaging test in suspected stroke, and what is its purpose?
back: NCCT (non-contrast CT) head, to rule out hemorrhage

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common location of hemorrhagic stroke, and what condition is it associated with?
back: The putamen; associated with hypertension

subject: Neurology
front: What clinical sign suggests internal capsule involvement in hemorrhagic stroke?
back: Dense hemiplegia

subject: Neurology
front: What is the treatment for hemorrhagic stroke, and what caution should be observed?
back: Blood pressure control, while avoiding rapid overcorrection since this may reduce cerebral perfusion

subject: Neurology
front: What is the investigation of choice for ischemic stroke, and what does it show?
back: MRI with DWI (diffusion-weighted imaging), which shows diffusion restriction

subject: Neurology
front: What does diffusion restriction on MRI in ischemic stroke reflect pathophysiologically?
back: Na+/K+ pump failure, causing cytotoxic edema

subject: Neurology
front: What is the treatment for ischemic stroke within the 4.5-hour window period?
back: Thrombolysis with IV Alteplase

subject: Neurology
front: What is the dosing regimen for IV Alteplase used in thrombolysis?
back: 0.9 mg/kg, given as a 10% bolus followed by a 90% infusion over 1 hour

subject: Neurology
front: What blood pressure level is a contraindication to thrombolysis?
back: BP greater than 185/110 mmHg

subject: Neurology
front: What are the time-based contraindications to thrombolysis related to recent surgery, GI bleed, and myocardial infarction?
back: Major surgery within 2 weeks, GI bleed within 3 weeks, and recent myocardial infarction (along with recent head injury/ICH and bleeding diathesis)

subject: Neurology
front: What NCCT finding is a relative contraindication to thrombolysis, and why?
back: Greater than 1/3 MCA territory infarct on NCCT, due to a higher risk of hemorrhagic transformation

subject: Neurology
front: What ASPECTS score indicates a larger infarct size and higher risk with thrombolysis?
back: An ASPECTS score of 4 or less (a lower score indicates a larger infarct size)

subject: Neurology
front: What is the management approach for ischemic stroke presenting beyond the 4.5-hour window?
back: CT angiography plus CT perfusion imaging

subject: Neurology
front: Under what two conditions is mechanical thrombectomy performed for ischemic stroke beyond the window period?
back: Large vessel occlusion, or a viable penumbra (reduced blood flow but normal blood volume due to dilated arteries)

subject: Neurology
front: Does the middle cerebral artery participate in the Circle of Willis, and what structure does it enter?
back: No, it does not participate in the Circle of Willis; it enters the lateral sulcus

subject: Neurology
front: What structures does the anterior choroidal artery supply?
back: The internal capsule and basal ganglia

subject: Neurology
front: What is the origin of the posterior communicating artery, and what circulation is it part of?
back: It is a direct branch of the ICA (internal carotid artery), and is part of the anterior circulation

subject: Neurology
front: Between which two arteries does the 3rd cranial nerve run?
back: Between the posterior cerebral artery (PCA) and the superior cerebellar artery

subject: Neurology
front: Through which anatomical canal does the labyrinthine (internal auditory) artery run?
back: The internal acoustic meatus

subject: Neurology
front: What is the most common location for a Berry (saccular) aneurysm?
back: The junction of the ACA and ACOM (anterior communicating artery)

subject: Neurology
front: What visual field defect can result from an ACA/ACOM aneurysm compressing the optic chiasma?
back: Bitemporal hemianopia (BTHA)

subject: Neurology
front: Which Circle of Willis aneurysm locations are most likely to rupture?
back: Aneurysms at the PCA and PCOM (posterior communicating artery)

subject: Neurology
front: What cranial nerve palsy results from a PCA/PCOM aneurysm, and what eye finding does it cause?
back: 3rd cranial nerve palsy, causing an ipsilateral "down and out" eye

subject: Neurology
front: What complication results from superior sagittal sinus (SSS) thrombosis, and by what mechanism?
back: Communicating hydrocephalus, due to reduced CSF absorption at the arachnoid granulations

subject: Neurology
front: How does a choroid plexus papilloma cause hydrocephalus, in contrast to SSS thrombosis?
back: It causes hydrocephalus via CSF overproduction, rather than by obstruction

subject: Neurology
front: What does the inferior sagittal sinus join, and what structure does it form?
back: It joins the vein of Galen to form the straight sinus

subject: Neurology
front: What two structures combine to form the vein of Galen (great cerebral vein)?
back: The internal cerebral vein and the vein of Rosenthal

subject: Neurology
front: What four sinuses meet at the confluence of sinuses, and what mnemonic describes them?
back: Transverse sinus, Occipital sinus, Straight sinus, and Superior sagittal sinus; mnemonic "TOSS"

subject: Neurology
front: What is the venous drainage pathway following the confluence of sinuses?
back: Confluence of sinuses -> transverse sinus -> sigmoid sinus -> internal jugular vein (IJV)

subject: Neurology
front: What imaging sign on CT venography indicates superior sagittal sinus thrombosis?
back: The empty delta sign

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of an Anterior Cerebral Artery (ACA) stroke?
back: Contralateral weakness and sensory loss with the lower limb more affected than the upper limb, urinary incontinence, and behavioral/personality changes (due to frontal lobe involvement)

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of a Middle Cerebral Artery (MCA) stroke?
back: Contralateral weakness and sensory loss affecting the face and upper limb

subject: Neurology
front: What deficit occurs with dominant parietal lobe involvement in an MCA stroke?
back: Aphasia

subject: Neurology
front: What deficit occurs with non-dominant parietal lobe involvement in an MCA stroke?
back: Hemi-spatial neglect

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of a Posterior Cerebral Artery (PCA) stroke?
back: Contralateral homonymous hemianopia, Anton syndrome (denial of blindness), and alexia without agraphia (in the dominant hemisphere)

subject: Neurology
front: What is Anton syndrome?
back: Denial of blindness, seen in PCA stroke

subject: Neurology
front: What is alexia without agraphia, and in which hemisphere does the causative lesion occur?
back: The ability to write but not read; occurs with a lesion in the dominant hemisphere in PCA stroke

subject: Neurology
front: What is the clinical presentation of a thalamic stroke?
back: Contralateral hemisensory loss, followed by burning, severe pain

subject: Neurology
front: What is Dejerine-Roussy syndrome?
back: The thalamic pain syndrome seen after thalamic stroke

subject: Neurology
front: What is stereognosis, and which lobe's function does it test?
back: The ability to identify an object by touching it; it tests parietal lobe function

subject: Neurology
front: What is graphesthesia, and which lobe's function does it test?
back: The ability to recognize tracings drawn on the skin; it tests parietal lobe function

subject: Neurology
front: What is simultagnosia, and where is the responsible lesion located?
back: Inability to perceive multiple objects together; the lesion is located in the parieto-occipital region

subject: Neurology
front: What is prosopagnosia, and where is the responsible lesion located?
back: Inability to perceive known faces; the lesion is located in the temporo-occipital region

subject: Neurology
front: In the "multiples of 12" scheme, which cranial nerve numbers correspond to the midbrain, pons, and medulla?
back: Midbrain: cranial nerves 3 and 4; Pons: cranial nerves 5,6,7,8; Medulla: cranial nerves 9,10,11,12

subject: Neurology
front: What structures make up the "M" (medial) group affected in brainstem strokes?
back: The MLF (causing INO), the corticospinal tract (causing motor weakness), and the medial lemniscus

subject: Neurology
front: What structures make up the "S" (lateral) group affected in brainstem strokes?
back: Sensory fibers (spinothalamic tract), the spinocerebellar tract, and the sympathetic column (causing Horner's syndrome)

subject: Neurology
front: Which cranial nerve is involved in midbrain stroke syndromes, and what pupillary finding results?
back: The 3rd cranial nerve; results in an ipsilateral "down and out" pupil

subject: Neurology
front: What clinical feature results from red nucleus involvement in a midbrain stroke?
back: Hyperkinesia

subject: Neurology
front: What clinical feature results from superior cerebellar peduncle involvement in a midbrain stroke?
back: Ataxia

subject: Neurology
front: With which major artery territory are midbrain stroke syndromes classically associated?
back: The posterior cerebral artery (PCA)

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of Weber syndrome, and which structure alone is involved?
back: Contralateral hemiplegia plus an ipsilateral "down and out" pupil; involves only the 3rd cranial nerve

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of Benedikt syndrome, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
back: Contralateral hyperkinesis, chorea, and tremor, plus an ipsilateral "down and out" pupil; involves the 3rd cranial nerve plus the red nucleus; mnemonic "Redhead"

subject: Neurology
front: What are the clinical features of Nothnagel syndrome, and what mnemonic helps recall it?
